content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
TO: 
Advice File 
J 
Date: 
May 19, 1982 
File No. M-82-092 
FROM: 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Robert E. Leidigh 
SUBJECT: 
Telephone Advice re Legally Required Participation 
Absence and Use of Alternates 
I received a telephone calIon 5/13/82 from Curtis Coleman, 
attorney with the Southwest Coastal Airbasin District. 
The facts 
and his question are as follows: 
1. 
The District has a quasi-judicial hearing board, composed 
of 1 attorney member, 1 doctor member, 1 engineer member and 2 
public members. 
The three professional members each have alternates 
who can function in their place. 
A quorum is 3 members of the 
5-member board and 3 votes are required for any action to be taken. 
2. 
Chevron Oil is seeking a variance regarding its violation 
of air pollution standards. 
It has obtained an emergeny variance 
from 1 board member (per statutory provision) pending a hearing by 
the full board. 
The board will meet nest on May 19. 
3. 
The doctor member has an investment in Chevron Oil of more 
than $1,000 and must disqualify himself. 
His alternate is on 
vacation for 2 months, out-of-state. 
4. 
One of the public members also has an investment in 
Chevron Oil of more than $1,000 and must disqualify himself. 
He has 
no alternate. 
5. 
The engineer member is on vacation for 3 weeks. 
His 
alternate has na investment in Chevron Oil of more than $1,000 and 
must disqualify himself. 
The question is whether the doctrine of "legally required 
participation" applies and the Hudson rule should be followed to 
permit a quorum to function at the May 19 meeting. 
We discussed this as an add-on question at the May 13 Opinion 
Request Meeting and agreed that the doctrine does not apply and that 
the Hudson rule does not apply. 
The absence of the-engineer and the 
doctor's alternate are the same as absences due to illness, etc., 
and will not suffice to invoke the doctrine and, hence, the rule. 
I advis 
Mr. Coleman of our adv 
and he indicated 
tone 
r could pr 
ex 
the emergency variance under 
se 
circumstances and that they would await the return of the others 
before the full board would act. 
REL:plh

sections.question: The question is whether the doctrine of "legally required participation" applies and the Hudson rule should be followed to permit a quorum to function at the May 19 meeting.
sections.conclusion: We discussed this as an add-on question at the May 13 Opinion Request Meeting and agreed that the doctrine does not apply and that the Hudson rule does not apply. The absence of the-engineer and the doctor's alternate are the same as absences due to illness, etc., and will not suffice to invoke the doctrine and, hence, the rule.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the "legally required participation" doctrine and Hudson rule permit a hearing board to function with a reduced quorum when board members and their alternates must disqualify due to conflicts of interest or are unavailable due to vacation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the legally required participation doctrine and Hudson rule do not apply. Absences due to vacation are treated the same as absences due to illness and cannot invoke the doctrine to allow reduced quorum operations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a memorandum documenting telephone advice rather than a formal written advice letter. Some text appears degraded ('advis' for 'advised', 'tone' fragments), but the question and conclusion are clearly identifiable. The document represents FPPC's response to legal question posed by an outside attorney.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The question is whether the doctrine of "legally required participation" applies and the Hudson rule should be followed to permit a quorum to function at the May 19 meeting.

CONCLUSION: We discussed this as an add-on question at the May 13 Opinion Request Meeting and agreed that the doctrine does not apply and that the Hudson rule does not apply. The absence of the-engineer and the doctor's alternate are the same as absences due to illness, etc., and will not suffice to invoke the doctrine and, hence, the rule.

The facts and his question are as follows: 1. The District has a quasi-judicial hearing board, composed of 1 attorney member, 1 doctor member, 1 engineer member and 2 public members. The three professional members each have alternates who can function in their place. A quorum is 3 members of the 5-member board and 3 votes are required for any action to be taken. 2. Chevron Oil is seeking a variance regarding its violation of air pollution standards. It has obtained an emergeny variance from 1 board member (per statutory provision) pending a hearing by the full board. The board will meet nest on May 19. 3. The doctor member has an investment in Chevron Oil of more than $1,000 and must disqualify himself. His alternate is on vacation for 2 months, out-of-state. 4. One of the public members also has an investment in Chevron Oil of more than $1,000 and must disqualify himself. He has no alternate. 5. The engineer member is on vacation for 3 weeks. His alternate has na investment in Chevron Oil of more than $1,000 and must disqualify himself. The question is whether the doctrine of "legally required participation" applies and the Hudson rule should be followed to permit a quorum to function at the May 19 meeting. We discussed this as an add-on question at the May 13 Opinion Request Meeting and agreed that the doctrine does not apply and that the Hudson rule does not apply. The absence of the-engineer and the doctor's alternate are the same as absences due to illness, etc., and will not suffice to invoke the doctrine and, hence, the rule. I advis Mr. Coleman of our adv and he indicated tone r could pr ex the emergency variance under se circumstances and that they would await the return of the others before the full board would act. REL:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC telephone advice regarding whether a quasi-judicial hearing board can function with reduced membership when members must disqualify due to Chevron Oil investments and alternates are unavailable. The FPPC advised that the legally required participation doctrine does not apply in these circumstances.
